Mild swelling in feet, ankles, and sometimes hands occurs, especially in warm weather or at the end of the day. Relaxin loosens ligaments and joints, contributing to back pain and foot size changes. The center of gravity shifts further forward, affecting balance. Varicose veins may develop in the legs or vulva. Belly skin may feel stretched and itchy вЂ” moisturizing helps comfort though doesn't prevent stretch marks. The increasingly tangible connection through movement often deepens maternal bonding, and you may think more concretely about labor and birth.
nutritionTips: Continue a balanced, nutrient-dense diet with emphasis on iron, calcium, protein, and omega-3 DHA. Iron-rich foods вЂ” lean red meat, poultry, fish, legumes, spinach, fortified cereals вЂ” paired with vitamin C at every meal. Calcium (1,200-1,500 mg) supports continuing ossification. Protein (71-80 grams) supports fetal growth and placental development. Omega-3 DHA from fatty fish supports the rapidly developing brain. Choline-rich foods like eggs are important for brain development and myelination. Fiber-rich foods вЂ” whole grains, legumes, fruits, vegetables, nuts, seeds вЂ” manage constipation. Hydration is increasingly important вЂ” 10 to 12 glasses of water daily supports blood volume, amniotic fluid, and helps manage swelling. Reduce sodium if swelling is bothersome, but don't severely restrict salt. Small, frequent meals help with heartburn.
vitaminRecommendations: Continue your prenatal vitamin. Iron (27 mg) is critically important as blood volume peaks; take consistently. If iron studies indicate deficiency, your provider may prescribe additional supplementation with vitamin C, away from calcium. Calcium from diet and supplements should reach 1,200 to 1,500 mg, spaced from iron. Omega-3 DHA (200-300 mg) supports brain and visual system development. Choline (450 mg) supports brain maturation and myelination вЂ” eggs are the best dietary source. Vitamin D (600-2,000 IU based on serum levels) supports bone health and immunity. Magnesium (350-360 mg) supports muscle function and may help with leg cramps and sleep. Iodine (150-220 mcg) supports fetal thyroid function. Continue low-dose aspirin if prescribed.
symptomsCommon: Fetal movements are now strong and regular вЂ” you should feel movement daily. An emerging pattern of activity and rest becomes apparent. Movements become more distinct вЂ” you may tell the difference between kicks, rolls, and hiccups. The growing belly continues causing back pain and sleep difficulties вЂ” a pregnancy pillow and sleeping on your left side help. Swelling in feet and ankles may increase; elevate feet, avoid prolonged standing, and wear comfortable shoes. Heartburn and indigestion often worsen as the uterus presses on the stomach. Eat smaller meals and avoid lying down for 2 to 3 hours after eating. Leg cramps, nasal congestion, and increased vaginal discharge persist. Some women develop varicose veins or hemorrhoids. Braxton Hicks contractions вЂ” irregular, painless uterine tightening вЂ” may begin. Mood is typically positive, and many women enjoy the increased energy and visible pregnancy.

babyDevelopment: The fetus measures 275 to 285 millimeters head to heel and weighs about 450 grams, roughly the size of a coconut. This marks the lower boundary of viability: if born at 22 weeks with the highest-level neonatal intensive care, survival has a small but real chance, though the risk of severe complications is high. Each additional week dramatically improves outcomes. The brain is entering extremely rapid growth, increasing six-fold in size between now and birth. Neurons migrate, form connections, and myelinate. Senses continue refining: the fetus can hear a wide range of sounds, including your voice, music, and ambient noise. Research shows newborns recognize their mother's voice and even specific songs or stories heard in utero. Touch is well-developed вЂ” the fetus explores by touching its face, grasping the umbilical cord, and pushing against uterine walls. Eyebrows and eyelashes have formed. The lips are well-defined and sensitive; sucking motions are practiced frequently. The pancreas develops islets of Langerhans for insulin and glucagon production. The liver begins processing bilirubin.
motherChanges: The uterus is about 2 centimeters above the navel and grows steadily. Weight gain may be 12 to 18 pounds. Fetal movements are strong and unmistakable; you may see your belly move or ripple as the baby kicks and stretches. The cardiovascular system works at peak capacity вЂ” blood volume is 45 to 50 percent above pre-pregnancy levels. Resting heart rate remains elevated, and breathlessness occurs with less exertion. The increasing uterine size causes physical discomfort: back pain, rib pain, sleep difficulty. Sleeping on your left side with a pregnancy pillow optimizes blood flow and comfort. Braxton Hicks contractions вЂ” irregular, painless uterine tightening вЂ” are normal practice for labor but should not be painful, regular, or intensifying. Swelling increases. Skin may feel itchy and stretched. Some women develop PUPPP вЂ” an itchy pregnancy rash on the abdomen. If itching is severe, especially on palms and soles, contact your provider as this can indicate cholestasis of pregnancy. Increasing viability brings reassurance.
nutritionTips: Continue a balanced diet emphasizing iron, calcium, protein, and omega-3 DHA. With the brain growing six-fold between now and birth, DHA from fatty fish and algae is particularly important вЂ” salmon, sardines, or trout 2 to 3 times per week. Iron-rich foods remain crucial as blood volume peaks; combine with vitamin C. Calcium from dairy, fortified foods, and leafy greens supports bone ossification. Protein (71-80 grams) supports rapid fetal growth. Eggs provide high-quality protein and choline. Complex carbohydrates from whole grains, sweet potatoes, and legumes sustain energy. Fresh fruits and vegetables supply antioxidants and fiber. Hydration at 10 glasses of water daily manages swelling, supports amniotic fluid, and aids digestion. Small, frequent meals help with heartburn. If gestational diabetes screening hasn't been done (typically weeks 24-28), continue your normal diet.
vitaminRecommendations: Continue your prenatal vitamin. Iron (27 mg) is critically important as blood volume reaches peak. Your provider should continue monitoring hemoglobin and ferritin. If iron deficiency is present, therapeutic supplementation beyond the prenatal dose will be recommended. Omega-3 DHA (200-300 mg) is especially important during exponential brain growth. Choline (450 mg) supports brain development and myelination. Calcium should reach 1,200 to 1,500 mg through diet and supplements, taken separately from iron. Vitamin D (600-2,000 IU) supports calcium utilization and immunity. Magnesium (350-360 mg) helps with muscle cramps and sleep. Iodine (150-220 mcg) supports thyroid function. If at risk for preterm birth, your provider may discuss progesterone supplementation (vaginal suppositories or injections) based on history and cervical length. Continue low-dose aspirin if prescribed.
symptomsCommon: Fetal movements are strong and consistent вЂ” you should feel the baby move daily. Some women notice a pattern of increased evening or post-meal activity. The growing belly causes significant back pain and sleep difficulties вЂ” a pregnancy pillow, left-side sleeping, and avoiding lying flat on the back improve comfort. Braxton Hicks contractions may become noticeable вЂ” irregular and painless, not true labor. Swelling in feet and ankles is common; elevate feet when possible. Heartburn often worsens; eat smaller meals and avoid lying down after eating. Shortness of breath continues. Varicose veins, hemorrhoids, and leg cramps are common. Stretch marks appear. Nasal congestion persists. Some women experience mild anxiety about labor and birth; childbirth classes and discussions with your provider are helpful.

babyDevelopment: The fetus measures 285 to 300 millimeters head to heel and weighs about 530 grams, roughly the size of a grapefruit. A critical development occurs: the lungs begin to produce surfactant, a lipoprotein substance that coats the inner surface of the alveoli (tiny air sacs). Surfactant reduces surface tension and prevents the alveoli from collapsing after each breath вЂ” without it, breathing is impossible. This is a crucial viability milestone, as respiratory distress syndrome (RDS) from surfactant deficiency is one of the most serious complications of prematurity. The skin becomes increasingly opaque as subcutaneous fat deposits increase, though the fetus is still lean and somewhat wrinkled. The face is almost fully formed, just thinner. The pancreas develops rapidly, with islet cells producing insulin. The inner ear vestibular system completes development, giving the fetus a sense of motion вЂ” dancing, rocking, or riding in a car is sensed. Rapid eye movements during sleep indicate REM sleep. The fetus has a regular activity-rest cycle. Loud noises may cause startling. In multiple pregnancies, fetuses can sense each other and may interact.
motherChanges: The uterus is about 4 centimeters (1.5 inches) above the navel. Weight gain may be 13 to 20 pounds. Fetal movements are daily, regular, and visible from the outside вЂ” partners can easily feel movement. The body works at peak capacity: blood volume is at or near maximum, cardiac output is significantly elevated, and metabolic rate is at its highest. As the second trimester draws toward a close, third-trimester discomforts begin. Swelling in feet, ankles, hands, and sometimes face is common. Back pain and pelvic pressure increase. Braxton Hicks contractions become stronger and more frequent вЂ” irregular, non-painful, resolving with rest, hydration, or position change. Colostrum may leak вЂ” your body is preparing for breastfeeding. Some women develop carpal tunnel syndrome вЂ” numbness, tingling, or pain in hands from fluid retention compressing the median nerve. Physical discomforts can be challenging; it's normal to feel both excited about the baby and ready for pregnancy to end.
nutritionTips: Continue a balanced diet. With surfactant production beginning, nutrition supporting lung development is valuable вЂ” omega-3 fatty acids DHA and EPA have anti-inflammatory effects that may support lung maturation. Eat fatty fish 2 to 3 times per week. Protein (71-80 grams) continues to be crucial. Iron-rich foods remain essential, paired with vitamin C. Calcium (1,200-1,500 mg) supports the hardening skeleton and helps regulate blood pressure. Vitamin C-rich foods вЂ” citrus, bell peppers, strawberries, kiwi, broccoli вЂ” support immune function and iron absorption. If swelling is bothersome, ensure adequate water intake (paradoxically, hydration helps reduce fluid retention) and moderate sodium. Small, frequent meals help with heartburn. If gestational diabetes screening is scheduled (weeks 24-28), follow your provider's instructions.
vitaminRecommendations: Continue your prenatal vitamin. Iron (27 mg) is critically important when blood volume is at or near peak. Ensure consistent daily supplementation. If blood work indicates anemia, your provider will prescribe additional iron. Omega-3 DHA (200-300 mg) supports lung surfactant production as well as brain development. Choline (450 mg) supports ongoing brain maturation. Calcium (1,200-1,500 mg total) and vitamin D (600-2,000 IU) are essential for bone health and may play a role in blood pressure regulation. Magnesium (350-360 mg) helps with leg cramps, sleep, and constipation. Iodine (150-220 mcg) supports fetal thyroid function. If you have a history of preterm birth or a short cervix, your provider may prescribe progesterone supplementation. Continue low-dose aspirin if prescribed.
symptomsCommon: Fetal movements are strong, regular, and obvious вЂ” daily movement should be felt. Swelling (edema) is common in feet, ankles, and sometimes hands and face; elevating feet, staying hydrated, wearing comfortable shoes, and avoiding prolonged standing help. Report sudden or severe facial swelling to your provider as it can indicate preeclampsia. Back pain and pelvic pressure increase. Braxton Hicks contractions may become more frequent. True labor contractions are regular, increase in intensity, and don't resolve with rest or hydration вЂ” contact your provider if suspecting preterm labor. Heartburn, shortness of breath, and sleep difficulties are common. Leg cramps continue. Carpal tunnel symptoms (numbness, tingling) may develop. Varicose veins and hemorrhoids are common. Mood fluctuates with physical discomforts. Continue exercise but adjust intensity as needed.

babyDevelopment: The fetus measures 300 to 315 millimeters head to heel and weighs about 650 grams, roughly the size of a cantaloupe. This is an important viability milestone: survival outside the womb chances increase significantly, with survival rates of 40 to 70 percent at 24 weeks in advanced NICUs. However, the risk of long-term complications including respiratory issues, neurological problems, and developmental delays remains significant. The lungs continue producing surfactant, and the bronchial tree branching structure is nearly complete, though alveoli are still primitive. The brain undergoes massive development: the cortex forms six distinct layers, and synapses form at astonishing rates. Sleep and wake cycles include REM sleep. The face is almost identical to how it will look at birth, with eyebrows, eyelashes, and hair. The inner ear structures are fully developed for balance and spatial orientation. The grip reflex is strong вЂ” the fetus may grasp the umbilical cord. Nostrils, previously plugged, now open. The fetus practices breathing movements about 30 to 40 percent of the time, essential for lung development.
motherChanges: The uterus is about 5 centimeters (2 inches) above the navel. Weight gain may be 14 to 22 pounds. The top of the uterus reaches the ribcage, causing rib discomfort and shortness of breath as the diaphragm is compressed. Your body approaches peak pregnancy physiology: blood volume is near maximum, cardiac output is highest, and kidneys filter blood 50 percent faster than before pregnancy. The gestational diabetes screening is typically performed between weeks 24 and 28 вЂ” a routine test involving drinking a glucose solution and having blood drawn after one hour. If elevated, a follow-up glucose tolerance test is done. Some women experience increased anxiety around this test. Swelling in feet, ankles, and hands becomes more pronounced. Braxton Hicks contractions may be more frequent. Skin stretches further; stretch marks continue to develop. After birth, the hair cycle normalizes and extra hair is shed. Knowing your baby has achieved viability can be reassuring, though thoughts of premature delivery may cause anxiety вЂ” discuss concerns with your provider.
nutritionTips: Nutrition remains critically important. Iron needs are at their highest вЂ” anemia is most common in the late second and third trimesters. Incorporate iron-rich foods at every meal, always paired with vitamin C. Avoid tea and coffee with iron-rich meals as tannins inhibit absorption. Calcium from dairy, fortified foods, sardines, and leafy greens continues supporting bone development. The gestational diabetes screening is routine вЂ” eat normally in the days before unless your provider instructs otherwise. On the test day, follow directions, which may include fasting or eating a specific meal beforehand. Continue balanced meals with complex carbohydrates, lean protein, healthy fats, and fiber for steady blood sugar regardless of the screening. Omega-3 DHA from fatty fish supports lung and brain development. Protein (71-80 grams) supports ongoing growth. Hydration at 10 glasses of water daily.
vitaminRecommendations: Continue your prenatal vitamin. Iron (27 mg) is at peak importance вЂ” anemia in late pregnancy is associated with fatigue, reduced exercise tolerance, and increased risk of complications including preterm birth and low birth weight. Your provider should monitor hemoglobin and ferritin. If iron deficiency is found, therapeutic doses (60-120 mg elemental iron daily) are typically prescribed, separated from calcium and taken with vitamin C. Omega-3 DHA (200-300 mg) supports lung surfactant production and brain development. Choline (450 mg) continues supporting brain maturation. Calcium (1,200-1,500 mg total) and vitamin D (600-2,000 IU) are essential. Magnesium (350-360 mg) helps with leg cramps, sleep, and blood pressure. Iodine (150-220 mcg) supports thyroid function. Continue progesterone supplementation and low-dose aspirin if prescribed.
symptomsCommon: Fetal movements are strong, regular, and may be visible from the outside. You should feel movement daily, and some pattern may be noticeable. The uterus approaches the ribs, causing rib discomfort, shortness of breath, and difficulty eating large meals. Heartburn intensifies. Swelling in feet, ankles, and hands is common; monitor for sudden or severe swelling, particularly facial swelling, which can indicate preeclampsia. Back pain, pelvic pressure, and round ligament pain worsen. Braxton Hicks contractions are normal but should remain irregular and painless. Leg cramps occur, especially at night. Carpal tunnel numbness or tingling may develop. Sleep difficulties are common вЂ” pregnancy pillows, left-side sleeping, and a consistent bedtime routine help. Regular moderate exercise is still recommended and helps manage many symptoms.

babyDevelopment: The fetus measures 320 to 340 millimeters head to heel and weighs about 700 grams, roughly the size of an eggplant. Weight increases steadily with accelerating fat deposition, smoothing out the wrinkled appearance of previous weeks. Capillaries form, improving skin circulation and giving it a pinker hue. The eyelids, fused since early first trimester, can now open and close. The fetus begins blinking and may spend periods with eyes open. The retina can detect light and dark; pupils constrict and dilate in response. The fetus may turn toward or away from bright light and reacts to sudden illumination. The brain continues extraordinary growth: the cerebral cortex thickens, and fissures and convolutions become more defined. The fetus demonstrates a clear preference for the mother's voice, a recognition developed through bone conduction. Lung surfactant production continues, and pulmonary blood vessels proliferate. Nostrils are now fully open; breathing practice increases. Lung capillaries develop in preparation for the dramatic shift from placental oxygenation to air breathing at birth.
motherChanges: This is the final week of the second trimester. The uterus is about 6 centimeters (2.4 inches) above the navel, nearing the ribcage. Weight gain may be 15 to 24 pounds. The belly is large and prominent; the belly button may pop out to form an 'outie,' which returns to pre-pregnancy state after delivery. Bending over, tying shoes, and getting comfortable become more challenging. Blood volume is approximately 50 percent above pre-pregnancy, and cardiac output is near its peak. The heart works significantly harder, and you may feel palpitations after minimal exertion. Pressure on the vena cava when lying on your back causes lightheadedness вЂ” sleeping on your left side maximizes placental blood flow. Round ligament pain, pelvic girdle pain (symphysis pubis dysfunction), and sciatica may occur as relaxin loosens joints and the baby's weight increases. Stretch marks expand; skin itchiness increases. Some women feel anxiety entering the third trimester вЂ” concerns about labor, birth, and newborn care are normal. Discuss fears with your provider or support network.
nutritionTips: Continue a nutrient-dense diet with emphasis on iron, calcium, protein, and omega-3 DHA. Iron needs are at their peak вЂ” incorporate lean red meat, poultry, fish, legumes, spinach, and fortified cereals, always paired with vitamin C. Calcium (1,200-1,500 mg) supports the skeleton. Protein (71-80 grams) supports fetal growth and increasing maternal demands. Omega-3 DHA is particularly important as the brain enters exponential growth and lung capillaries develop. Choline from eggs supports brain development and myelination. Fiber-rich foods вЂ” whole grains, legumes, fruits with skin, vegetables вЂ” are essential for digestive health. Hydration is critical вЂ” 10 to 12 glasses of water daily supports blood volume, amniotic fluid, and helps reduce swelling and constipation. Small, frequent meals manage heartburn. Avoid alcohol, high-mercury fish, unpasteurized dairy, raw or undercooked animal products, and excessive caffeine.
vitaminRecommendations: Continue your prenatal vitamin. Iron (27 mg) is critically important вЂ” if iron studies show deficiency, your provider will prescribe therapeutic doses (60-120 mg elemental iron daily). Take with vitamin C, separate from calcium. Omega-3 DHA (200-300 mg) supports lung development, brain growth, and retinal maturation. Choline (450 mg) continues to be important for brain development. Calcium (1,200-1,500 mg total) and vitamin D (600-2,000 IU) support bone health and may help with blood pressure regulation. Magnesium (350-360 mg) helps with leg cramps, sleep, and constipation. Iodine (150-220 mcg) supports fetal thyroid function. If your glucose screening test results were elevated, you may need a 3-hour glucose tolerance test for definitive diagnosis. If diagnosed with gestational diabetes, you'll receive specific dietary guidance and glucose monitoring instructions.
symptomsCommon: The final week of the second trimester brings increasing physical discomforts. The growing belly makes sleeping, bending, and moving more challenging. Shortness of breath continues as the uterus presses against the diaphragm. Heartburn intensifies. Swelling in feet, ankles, and hands may worsen. Back pain and pelvic pressure are common вЂ” proper body mechanics, supportive shoes, and maternity support belts help. Braxton Hicks contractions become more frequent and stronger. Leg cramps continue. Carpal tunnel symptoms may be bothersome. Stretch marks continue appearing and may be itchy; moisturizing soothes the skin. Sleep disturbances are very common. Mood fluctuates between excitement about the baby and weariness with physical discomforts. This is a good time to take childbirth education classes, tour the hospital or birth center, and discuss birth preferences with your provider.

babySize: the size of a head of cauliflower
babyDevelopment: The fetus measures 330 to 355 millimeters head to heel and weighs about 800 grams, roughly the size of a head of cauliflower. Week 26 marks the beginning of the third trimester. The lungs undergo critical development: air sacs (alveoli) at the ends of bronchial tubes begin forming, and surfactant production increases. Pulmonary blood vessels proliferate, preparing lung tissue for the transition from placental oxygenation to air breathing. The fetus practices breathing movements about 30 to 40 percent of the time. The brain grows exponentially, with the cerebral cortex developing increasingly complex circuitry. Brain wave activity shows patterns similar to a newborn. The fetal eyes are open for significant periods, and the optic nerve nears maturity. The fetus distinguishes different tastes in amniotic fluid, and preferences may form based on the mother's diet вЂ” flavors from garlic, vanilla, and anise pass into amniotic fluid and influence later food acceptance. The immune system develops as bone marrow produces white blood cells, and the spleen contributes to immune function. Brown fat stores essential for newborn temperature regulation accumulate.
motherChanges: Welcome to the third trimester! The uterus is approximately 7 centimeters (2.8 inches) above the navel. Weight gain may be 16 to 24 pounds. The baby's movements are distinct and powerful вЂ” you may see limbs moving across your belly. The third trimester brings increased demands on your body. The growing uterus puts pressure on the diaphragm, stomach, and bladder. Shortness of breath, heartburn, and frequent urination are common. Blood volume is at or near its peak, and you may experience increased swelling and varicose veins. Sleep becomes more challenging as finding comfortable positions becomes difficult. Some women experience restless leg syndrome вЂ” uncomfortable sensations in the legs, especially at night, relieved by movement. Iron deficiency can contribute to restless legs, so discuss with your provider. Emotionally, the anticipation of meeting your baby grows, but physical discomforts can wear on your patience. This is a good time to finalize birth plans, pack a hospital bag, and complete childbirth preparation.
nutritionTips: Nutrition demands peak in the third trimester. Iron needs are at their absolute highest вЂ” iron deficiency anemia in the third trimester is associated with preterm birth, low birth weight, and increased maternal fatigue. Consume iron-rich foods with vitamin C at every meal. Calcium (1,200-1,500 mg) supports bone development and may help regulate blood pressure. The baby's brain continues growing exponentially through the third trimester вЂ” DHA omega-3 from fatty fish and fish oil is critical. Protein at 71 to 80 grams daily supports fetal growth and maternal tissue maintenance. Choline from eggs supports brain development. Fiber from whole grains, legumes, fruits, and vegetables manages constipation. Water intake of 10 to 12 glasses daily supports blood volume and reduces swelling. Small, frequent meals help with heartburn and energy. If diagnosed with gestational diabetes, follow your dietary plan carefully.
vitaminRecommendations: Continue your prenatal vitamin. Iron (27 mg) is critically important вЂ” many women need additional supplementation in the third trimester. Monitor for signs of anemia: extreme fatigue, pallor, shortness of breath, heart palpitations. Therapeutic iron (60-120 mg elemental iron daily) is often prescribed; take with vitamin C, away from calcium. Omega-3 DHA (200-300 mg) supports brain development and lung maturation. Choline (450 mg) supports ongoing brain development. Calcium (1,200-1,500 mg total) and vitamin D (600-2,000 IU) are essential. Magnesium (350-360 mg) helps with leg cramps, restless legs, sleep, and may support healthy blood pressure. Iodine (150-220 mcg) supports thyroid function. Ligament pain in the front of the pelvis (symphysis pubis dysfunction) may make walking, stairs, and turning in bed painful. Shortness of breath, heartburn, and rib discomfort persist. Swelling is common вЂ” differentiate normal edema from preeclampsia signs (sudden severe swelling, headache, vision changes, upper abdominal pain). Sleep remains elusive. Restless leg syndrome, carpal tunnel, and frequent urination disrupt sleep. Braxton Hicks contractions become more frequent. Hemorrhoids may be painful. The 'waddle' вЂ” the characteristic pregnancy gait from pelvic ligament relaxation вЂ” is common. Fatigue is significant. Emotional highs and lows are normal. Lean on your support system.

babyDevelopment: The fetus measures 440 to 460 millimeters head to heel and weighs about 2,200 grams, roughly the size of a cantaloupe. The lungs are approaching maturity вЂ” most fetuses born at 34 weeks have a well-developed respiratory system requiring minimal or no support. Surfactant production is adequate for independent breathing, though some 34-weekers may still experience mild respiratory difficulties as their lungs are not yet fully mature. The central nervous system continues maturing, and coordination of sucking, swallowing, and breathing вЂ” essential for feeding вЂ” improves daily. The immune system receives a significant boost from maternal antibody transfer; the fetus has a functional, though immature, immune response. Fat layers thicken, accounting for 8 to 10 percent of total body weight (approaching 15 to 16 percent by full term), serving as crucial energy reserve and insulation. The digestive system is fully developed and ready for milk feeding; meconium accumulates. The testes have descended into the scrotum in most male fetuses. Sleep-wake cycles are predictable; you may notice your baby's activity pattern. Lanugo has almost completely disappeared, with only small patches on shoulders and back. Vernix caseosa thins naturally. Fingernails have grown beyond the fingertips.
motherChanges: The uterus is about 14 centimeters (5.6 inches) above the navel. Weight gain may be 24 to 36 pounds. The baby may begin to engage (drop into the pelvis) around now, especially in first pregnancies. Engagement вЂ” also called lightening вЂ” brings some breathing relief as the baby moves lower, but increases pelvic pressure, urinary frequency, and sometimes hemorrhoid discomfort. If this is not your first pregnancy, the baby may not engage until labor begins. Prenatal visits may increase to weekly. Your provider monitors blood pressure, urine, fetal position, and sometimes begins cervical checks to assess dilation and effacement. Emotionally, the impending birth becomes very real. You may feel a mix of excitement to meet your baby joined with anxiety about labor and delivery. The nesting instinct often peaks вЂ” an urge to organize, clean, and prepare the home. Leverage this energy but be careful not to overexert yourself.
nutritionTips: Continue a nutrient-dense diet. Iron needs remain at peak вЂ” the baby is building iron stores that will last up to 6 months. Every meal should include iron-rich foods with vitamin C. Calcium (1,200-1,500 mg) supports final bone mineralization. Protein at 71 to 80 grams daily supports continued growth and tissue maintenance. Omega-3 DHA is critical for the final brain development push before birth. Eat fatty fish 2 to 3 times weekly. Choline from eggs supports brain maturation. Fiber-rich foods manage severe late-pregnancy constipation. Complex carbohydrates provide sustained energy. Small, frequent meals (5 to 6) manage heartburn. Water at 10 to 12 glasses daily. Some evidence suggests eating 6 dates per day in the final weeks of pregnancy may promote cervical ripening вЂ” discuss with your provider.
vitaminRecommendations: Continue your prenatal vitamin. Iron (27 mg minimum; therapeutic doses of 60-120 mg if anemic) is critically important вЂ” continue through delivery and into the postpartum period. Omega-3 DHA (200-300 mg) supports the final prenatal brain development phase. Research suggests DHA supplementation in late pregnancy may reduce the risk of preterm birth and improve infant cognitive outcomes. Choline (450 mg) supports brain maturation. Calcium (1,200-1,500 mg) and vitamin D (600-2,000 IU) are essential. Magnesium (350-360 mg) helps with leg cramps, restless legs, sleep, and may help prevent preterm contractions. Iodine (150-220 mcg) supports thyroid function. Schedule Tdap if not yet done (weeks 27-36). GBS screening typically at weeks 35-37. Continue gestational diabetes management, progesterone, and low-dose aspirin as prescribed.
symptomsCommon: Late pregnancy symptoms are intense. Fetal movements remain important to monitor; the character may change (more rolling, stretching) but frequency should not decrease. Engagement (lightening) may occur, bringing breathing relief but increased pelvic pressure, urinary frequency, and waddling. Back pain and pelvic girdle pain may be severe вЂ” maternity belts, chiropractic care, and prenatal massage can help. Heartburn may improve if the baby drops, reducing stomach pressure. But for many, it persists. Swelling continues вЂ” monitor carefully for preeclampsia signs. Sleep is severely disrupted. Restless leg syndrome, carpal tunnel, and frequent urination persist. Braxton Hicks contractions become more frequent and may be more intense. Hemorrhoids and varicose veins may worsen. Fatigue is profound. Emotional fluctuations are normal. Practice labor positions and breathing techniques.

babySize: the size of a small melon
babyDevelopment: The fetus measures 450 to 470 millimeters head to heel and weighs about 2,400 grams, roughly the size of a small melon. Space in the uterus is becoming tight, and you may notice changes in fetal movement quality вЂ” fewer dramatic kicks, more squirming, rolling, and pushing. The grasp reflex is well-developed and can firmly grasp objects; after birth, this reflex will be strong enough to support the baby's own weight briefly. The kidneys are fully mature, producing urine that maintains amniotic fluid volume. The lungs are nearly fully mature, and most babies born at 35 weeks breathe independently without assistance. The brain develops rapidly, with the cerebral cortex forming increasingly complex neural circuits. The fetus blinks in response to light and startles in response to loud sounds. Fat accumulation continues вЂ” weight is approximately 15 percent fat, providing energy stores and insulation for temperature regulation after birth. The head circumference has increased significantly to accommodate the growing brain, and the skull bones remain soft and separated at the suture lines to allow molding during delivery. The umbilical cord transports nutrients and oxygen efficiently, though the placenta has reached maximum functional capacity and will begin aging naturally. The fetal position is well-established, and spontaneous turning becomes less likely.
motherChanges: The uterus is at its highest point вЂ” approximately 15 centimeters (6 inches) above the navel, just below the ribcage вЂ” or slightly lower if the baby has engaged. Weight gain may be 25 to 37 pounds. The baby's position may determine your comfort: if still high, breathing and heartburn are severe; if engaged, walking and sitting become difficult. Prenatal visits are now weekly. Your provider performs group B streptococcus (GBS) screening this week or next вЂ” a simple vaginal and rectal swab that tests for a common bacterium. About 25 percent of women carry GBS; if positive, you'll receive IV antibiotics during labor to protect the baby from infection. Cervical checks may begin to assess dilation and effacement, though these are optional and not predictive of when labor will start. Emotionally, the countdown is real вЂ” about 5 weeks to the due date. You may feel a mix of excitement, anxiety, and readiness. It's normal to worry about labor pain, your baby's health, and the transition to parenthood.
nutritionTips: Continue a nutrient-dense diet. Your caloric needs are at their highest вЂ” approximately 450 to 500 extra calories per day in the third trimester, but these should come from nutrient-dense foods, not empty calories. Iron needs are at peak вЂ” the baby's final iron stores are being built. Pair iron-rich foods with vitamin C at every meal. Calcium (1,200-1,500 mg) supports bone mineralization. Protein at 71 to 80 grams daily supports final weight gain. Omega-3 DHA is critical for brain and retinal maturation вЂ” eat fatty fish 2 to 3 times weekly. Choline from eggs supports the developing brain. Fiber-rich foods manage constipation. Small, frequent meals (5 to 6) manage heartburn. Water at 10 to 12 glasses daily. If you're eating dates (6 per day) to promote cervical ripening, continue вЂ” some research supports this practice for reducing the need for labor induction.
vitaminRecommendations: Continue your prenatal vitamin. Iron (27 mg minimum; therapeutic doses if anemic) is critically important вЂ” continue through delivery and into the postpartum period. Postpartum anemia significantly impacts recovery, energy, and breastfeeding success. Omega-3 DHA (200-300 mg) supports final brain and retinal maturation вЂ” some research suggests DHA in late pregnancy may contribute to improved infant cognitive outcomes. Choline (450 mg) supports brain development. Calcium (1,200-1,500 mg) and vitamin D (600-2,000 IU) are essential. Magnesium (350-360 mg) helps with leg cramps, restless legs, sleep, and may support healthy blood pressure. Iodine (150-220 mcg) supports thyroid function. Complete GBS screening this week or next. Ensure Tdap vaccination is done (weeks 27-36). Continue gestational diabetes management, progesterone, and low-dose aspirin as prescribed.
symptomsCommon: The final weeks of pregnancy bring intense physical demands. Fetal movements should be monitored вЂ” any significant decrease warrants immediate medical evaluation. Movements may feel like squirms and rolls rather than sharp kicks as space decreases. Breathing may be slightly better if the baby has engaged (dropped), but pelvic pressure increases. Back pain, pelvic girdle pain, and the 'waddle' intensify. Heartburn may improve if the baby has engaged; if not, it remains severe. Swelling continues вЂ” monitor for preeclampsia. Sleep is profoundly disrupted. Restless leg syndrome, carpal tunnel, and urinary frequency persist. Braxton Hicks contractions become more intense and may be difficult to distinguish from early labor. Hemorrhoids are common and may be painful. Fatigue is significant. Emotional fluctuations are normal вЂ” the anticipation and anxiety about labor and meeting your baby intensify. Practice labor techniques and prepare mentally.

babySize: the size of a small watermelon
babyDevelopment: The fetus measures 460 to 480 millimeters head to heel and weighs about 2,600 grams, roughly the size of a small watermelon. Pregnancy is now considered 'early term' (37 to 38 weeks and 6 days is 'early term,' and 39 to 40 weeks and 6 days is 'full term'). The fetus is essentially fully developed and could be born safely at any time. The lungs, liver, brain, and all major organ systems are ready for extrauterine life. The only remaining significant task is continued weight gain and final lung maturation. The fetus's face is now plump and rounded, with full cheeks characteristic of newborns. These fat pads in the cheeks provide structural support for sucking and help create the seal around the nipple during feeding. The suck-swallow-breathe coordination is well-practiced and ready for feeding. The remaining vernix caseosa sheds into the amniotic fluid, which becomes increasingly cloudy. Swallowed vernix contributes to the meconium accumulating in the intestines. The fetus continues receiving maternal antibodies, and the immune system is increasingly prepared for life outside the womb. For most fetuses, the head is engaged in the pelvis in preparation for delivery, though engagement may not occur until labor begins in first pregnancies.
motherChanges: The uterus is at its highest point and may now be slightly lower if the baby is engaging. Weight gain may be 25 to 37 pounds вЂ” weight gain often slows or plateaus in the final weeks. The baby is considered 'early term' by the end of this week. Prenatal visits are weekly. Your provider continues monitoring blood pressure, fetal heart rate, fetal position, and may perform cervical checks. If GBS screening last week was positive, note it вЂ” you'll need IV antibiotics during labor. Many women feel increasing pressure in the pelvis and rectum as the baby descends. Lightening (engagement) brings some breathing relief but increases lower-body discomfort. You may experience an increase in vaginal discharge, including the possible loss of the mucus plug вЂ” a thick, gelatinous blob of discharge, possibly blood-tinged, which may pass weeks before or just hours before labor. Losing the mucus plug does not necessarily mean labor is imminent. Emotionally, the anticipation is intense. You may feel 'done' with pregnancy and ready to meet your baby.
nutritionTips: Continue a nutrient-dense diet. Iron needs remain at peak вЂ” the baby's iron stores for the first 4 to 6 months of life are built now. Calcium (1,200-1,500 mg) supports final bone mineralization. Protein at 71 to 80 grams daily supports final growth. Omega-3 DHA continues supporting brain development. Choline from eggs supports brain maturation. Small, frequent meals (5 to 6 daily) manage heartburn and provide steady energy. Fiber-rich foods manage constipation, though bowel movements may change as the baby descends. Water at 10 to 12 glasses daily. Some evidence supports eating dates (6 per day) for cervical ripening вЂ” discuss with your provider. If you are GBS positive, no dietary changes are needed; it's managed with IV antibiotics during labor. Avoid foods that cause heartburn or indigestion. Consider meal prepping and freezing meals for the postpartum period вЂ” having nutritious food ready after birth is invaluable.
vitaminRecommendations: Continue your prenatal vitamin. Iron (27 mg minimum; therapeutic doses if anemic) is critical вЂ” continue through delivery and postpartum. Postpartum iron deficiency contributes significantly to fatigue, depression risk, and breastfeeding difficulties. Omega-3 DHA (200-300 mg) supports final brain development вЂ” consider continuing DHA through breastfeeding for ongoing infant brain support. Choline (450 mg) supports brain development. Calcium (1,200-1,500 mg) and vitamin D (600-2,000 IU) support maternal bone health postpartum, especially if breastfeeding. Magnesium (350-360 mg) helps with sleep, muscle cramps, and may support blood pressure. Iodine (150-220 mcg) supports thyroid function and will be needed for breastfeeding. Ensure Tdap is done. Continue gestational diabetes management, progesterone, and low-dose aspirin as prescribed. Some providers recommend continuing prenatal vitamins through breastfeeding for 6 months postpartum.
symptomsCommon: The final weeks bring unique symptoms. The baby is early term вЂ” birth at this point is considered safe, though 39 to 40 weeks is optimal. Fetal movement patterns remain important. The baby may engage (lightening), bringing breathing improvement but increased pelvic pressure, hemorrhoids, and urinary frequency. The mucus plug may pass вЂ” this is normal and does not always mean labor is near. 'Bloody show' вЂ” passage of blood-tinged mucus вЂ” can indicate cervical changes approaching labor. Back pain and pelvic pain are significant. Sleep is extremely challenging; rest whenever possible. Braxton Hicks contractions may be more intense and could be confused with early labor вЂ” true contractions are regular, progressively stronger, and do not resolve with rest or hydration. Swelling, carpal tunnel, and restless legs persist. Emotional fluctuations intensify вЂ” both the excitement to meet your baby and the anxiety about labor are completely normal.

babySize: the size of a medium watermelon
babyDevelopment: The fetus measures 470 to 490 millimeters head to heel and weighs about 2,800 grams, roughly the size of a medium watermelon. This is 'early term' вЂ” if labor begins spontaneously, it is not considered premature. The fetus is fully prepared for life outside the womb. The lungs are mature, surfactant production is adequate, and alveoli are well-developed and ready for gas exchange. The circulatory system is ready for the dramatic transition at birth: the foramen ovale and ductus arteriosus вЂ” fetal blood vessels that bypassed the lungs вЂ” will close within hours or days of birth as the baby takes their first breaths, establishing the independent circulatory pathway. The fetus continues accumulating fat at about 200 to 250 grams per week. The vernix caseosa has mostly dissolved into the now-cloudy amniotic fluid. Lanugo is almost entirely gone. The immune system has benefited from substantial maternal antibody transfer, providing protection against many infections. The brain and nervous system continue fine-tuning, a process that will continue for years after birth. The fetus demonstrates clear preferences, reacting to specific voices and music. Sleep-wake cycles are well-established; the fetus sleeps 80 to 90 percent of the time. The adrenal glands produce increasing cortisol, which matures organs and may play a role in labor initiation.
motherChanges: The uterus may be slightly lower now if the baby has engaged. Weight gain may be 25 to 37 pounds and often plateaus in the final weeks. Congratulations вЂ” your baby is now 'early term' and could arrive safely at any time. Labor could begin this week or the weeks ahead. The physical demands are significant. Walking, sitting, and sleeping all require strategy. Your provider checks fetal position, cervical dilation, and effacement at weekly visits. If the baby is breech at this stage, external cephalic version (ECV) may be offered as an attempt to turn the baby. About 3 to 4 percent of babies remain breech at term. Some women experience prodromal labor вЂ” contractions that are uncomfortable and somewhat regular but don't progress into active labor. This 'false labor' can last days and is physically and emotionally exhausting, though it may help prepare the cervix. Emotionally, you may feel ready, anxious, excited, overwhelmed, and impatient вЂ” often within the same hour. The final countdown has begun.
nutritionTips: Continue nutritious eating. Iron needs remain high through delivery вЂ” maintain iron-rich foods with vitamin C. Calcium (1,200-1,500 mg) supports bone health. Protein at 71 to 80 grams daily supports final preparations for delivery and breastfeeding. Omega-3 DHA consumption continues benefiting the baby and may provide anti-inflammatory benefits that support labor. Choline from eggs supports brain development. Light, easily digestible meals may be more comfortable вЂ” soups, smoothies, yogurt, toast, crackers, fruit. The stomach is compressed, and large meals cause significant heartburn. Continue eating dates if desired for cervical ripening support. Water at 10 to 12 glasses daily. Many women find their appetite decreases in the very final weeks as the stomach is compressed вЂ” eat small amounts frequently to maintain energy. Consider final meal prepping and freezing for postpartum. Pack snacks for the hospital: easily digestible options like granola bars, crackers, fruit pouches.
vitaminRecommendations: Continue your prenatal vitamin вЂ” iron levels heading into delivery significantly impact postpartum recovery. Iron (27 mg minimum; therapeutic doses of 60-120 mg if anemic) should be continued. Omega-3 DHA (200-300 mg) supports the baby at delivery and through breastfeeding. Choline (450 mg) supports the brain. Calcium (1,200-1,500 mg) and vitamin D (600-2,000 IU) maintain maternal bone health. Magnesium (350-360 mg) may help with sleep and muscle function heading toward labor. Iodine (150-220 mcg) will be essential for breastfeeding. GBS results should be known now вЂ” if positive, IV antibiotics during labor protect the baby. Continue gestational diabetes management, progesterone, and low-dose aspirin as prescribed вЂ” ask your provider when to stop low-dose aspirin (typically at 36-37 weeks or before delivery to reduce bleeding risk).
symptomsCommon: Early term: baby could arrive any day. Fetal movements remain essential to monitor вЂ” contact your provider immediately for any significant decrease. Discerning true labor from false labor: true contractions are regular, increase in intensity and frequency, and do not stop with rest or hydration. False labor (Braxton Hicks or prodromal labor) is irregular, doesn't consistently intensify, and may stop with rest or position change. The mucus plug may pass. Bloody show may occur. Some women experience nesting surges of energy close to labor вЂ” use this wisely without exhausting yourself. Back pain, pelvic pressure, and difficulty moving are significant. Heartburn may continue. Swelling, carpal tunnel, and restless legs persist. Sleep is elusive. Diarrhea or loose stools can occur in the days before labor as the body naturally clears the bowels. Emotional readiness varies вЂ” some women feel calm and ready, others anxious. Both are normal.

babySize: the size of a large watermelon
babyDevelopment: The fetus measures 480 to 500 millimeters head to heel and weighs about 3,000 grams, roughly the size of a large watermelon. The fetus is at or approaching birth weight and is fully mature. The lungs produce surfactant at optimal levels, the liver and kidneys filter waste independently, and the brain is ready for the flood of sensory input that will arrive at birth. All organ systems are prepared for the dramatic transition from intrauterine to extrauterine life. The head circumference is approximately 34 to 35 centimeters, and the skull bones remain separated at the sutures and fontanelles to allow molding during vaginal delivery вЂ” this molding is normal and temporary; the skull returns to a rounded shape within days. The fetus is quite crowded; you may feel feet under your ribs or the bottom pressing against your diaphragm. The baby's hair may be thick and long or just a fine fuzz; hair texture and color at birth often change during the first year. Nails extend beyond the fingertips in many fetuses, which can cause minor scratches on the face in utero or during delivery. The placenta, which has nourished the fetus for nearly nine months, shows signs of aging вЂ” calcifications, fibrin deposits, and decreased efficiency вЂ” though it continues to meet the fetus's needs adequately. The umbilical cord is approximately 50 to 60 centimeters in length and 2 centimeters in diameter.
motherChanges: The uterus is at its fullest, though it may be slightly lower if the baby has deeply engaged. Weight gain plateaus; some women even lose 1 to 2 pounds at the very end. Labor could begin at any moment. Your baby is fully mature and ready for birth. Weekly prenatal visits continue with cervical checks, blood pressure monitoring, and fetal heart rate assessment. Your provider discusses signs of labor, when to go to the hospital, and final birth plan details. Many women feel significant pelvic pressure and may have difficulty walking. The 'waddle' is pronounced. You may experience increased Braxton Hicks contractions or prodromal labor, which can be confusing and exhausting. The mucus plug may have passed already, or it may pass now. Some women lose the mucus plug in pieces over days, while others pass it all at once. Emotionally, you are likely oscillating between readiness, excitement, anxiety, and impatience. It's normal to feel 'done' and eager to meet your baby. Try to rest, conserve energy, and do gentle activities you enjoy.
nutritionTips: Continue light, nutritious eating. Iron remains important heading into delivery. Calcium supports bone health. Protein supports energy and recovery. Omega-3 DHA supports the baby through delivery and into breastfeeding. Easily digestible foods are most comfortable: soups, smoothies, yogurt, oatmeal, toast, eggs, fruit. Heartburn may mean very small, frequent meals. Water at 10 to 12 glasses daily. Some women find eating spicy foods stimulates labor, though there is limited scientific evidence вЂ” it's fine to try if tolerated, but don't force foods that cause discomfort. Raspberry leaf tea (1 to 3 cups per day) is traditionally used to tone the uterus and may support labor, though evidence is mixed вЂ” discuss with your provider. Avoid new or untested herbal supplements. If your appetite is minimal due to compression, focus on nutrient-dense liquids: smoothies with protein, greens, fruit, and nut butter; bone broth; full-fat yogurt drinks. Pack snacks for the hospital that are easy to digest during early labor.
vitaminRecommendations: Continue your prenatal vitamin through delivery and discuss postpartum supplementation with your provider. Iron is especially important вЂ” heading into delivery with healthy iron stores reduces the risks associated with postpartum hemorrhage and supports recovery. Continue iron supplementation as prescribed. Omega-3 DHA (200-300 mg) continues to support infant brain development and may benefit maternal mental health postpartum. Choline (450 mg) supports brain development. Calcium (1,200-1,500 mg) and vitamin D (600-2,000 IU) are essential for ongoing maternal bone health. Magnesium (350-360 mg) may support muscle function during labor and help with sleep. Iodine (150-220 mcg) will be essential for breastfeeding. Confirm with your provider whether to stop low-dose aspirin before delivery (typically by 36-37 weeks). GBS-positive mothers should have a plan for IV antibiotics during labor.
symptomsCommon: Full term: labor could begin at any time. Recognizing true labor: regular contractions that become progressively closer together, longer, and stronger; contractions that do not stop with rest, hydration, or position change; contractions that wrap from back to front. Time contractions вЂ” when they are 5 minutes apart, lasting 60 seconds, for at least 1 hour (the 5-1-1 rule), it's typically time to go to the hospital or birthing center, though follow your provider's specific guidance. Other labor signs: water breaking (rupture of membranes) вЂ” may be a gush or a trickle; bloody show; diarrhea or loose stools; nesting energy surge; lower back pain that doesn't resolve. False labor (prodromal labor) can last hours to days вЂ” rest when possible, stay hydrated, and contact your provider with concerns. The baby's movements should continue regularly until labor. Report any significant decrease immediately.

babySize: the size of a very large watermelon
babyDevelopment: The fetus measures 490 to 510 millimeters head to heel and weighs about 3,200 grams, roughly the size of a very large watermelon. This is considered 'full term' вЂ” the optimal time for delivery. The fetus has completed its intrauterine development and is ready for birth at any moment. The lungs are fully mature, with adequate surfactant covering the alveolar surfaces to prevent collapse after each breath. The fetal circulatory system is primed for the immediate transition at birth: the ductus arteriosus, ductus venosus, and foramen ovale will close within minutes to days, establishing the independent circulatory pathway. The skin is pink and smooth, with good subcutaneous fat coverage. Vernix caseosa remains only in skin folds вЂ” neck creases, armpits, and groin area. Lanugo has disappeared. The breast tissue, in both males and females, may be slightly enlarged from maternal hormones вЂ” temporary and will resolve after birth. The immune system is ready with maternal IgG antibodies providing passive immunity for the first months of life. The digestive system is primed for colostrum, the first milk perfectly matched to the newborn's nutritional and immunological needs. The fetus now has more than 300 bones вЂ” some will fuse after birth, reducing the adult count to 206. Movements may have noticeably decreased as the fetus has dropped into the pelvis.
motherChanges: Full term вЂ” your baby is at the optimal gestational age for delivery. The uterus is full-sized, and the baby has usually engaged deeply in the pelvis. Walking may be especially difficult with the baby's head low in the pelvis. Prenatal visits continue weekly. Your provider monitors cervical dilation and effacement, though you can be dilated for weeks before active labor begins. Some women are 3 to 4 centimeters dilated without being in active labor. The cervix also softens (ripens) and thins (effaces) in preparation. You may experience increased Braxton Hicks contractions, backache, pelvic pressure, and episodes of prodromal labor. Discerning real from false labor remains challenging вЂ” the key is progression. Real contractions increase in frequency, duration, and intensity. Emotionally, the waiting game is mentally taxing. It's normal to feel hyper-aware of every twinge and sensation, wondering if 'this is it.' Distraction is helpful вЂ” watch movies, spend time with loved ones, do gentle activities. Rest is crucial; if labor begins at night, starting labor well-rested is advantageous. The anticipation of meeting your baby can make patience difficult.
nutritionTips: Maintain light, nutritious eating. Labor requires significant energy вЂ” having fuel throughout early labor prevents exhaustion. Iron continues to be important heading into delivery to reduce risks associated with blood loss. Easily digestible foods are most comfortable. Complex carbohydrates вЂ” oatmeal, whole grain toast, crackers, pasta вЂ” provide sustained energy. Protein вЂ” eggs, yogurt, nut butter вЂ” supports stamina. Fruits provide quick energy and hydration. Smoothies combine multiple nutrients in an easily digestible form. Small amounts frequently tolerate better than large meals. Water at 10 to 12 glasses daily вЂ” dehydration can cause false contractions. Electrolyte drinks or coconut water support hydration. During early labor, light eating is generally safe (discuss your provider's policy вЂ” some hospitals restrict food during active labor). Pack hospital bag snacks: honey sticks, granola bars, crackers, fruit, electrolyte drinks. Continue or discontinue date consumption as you prefer. Avoid heavy, greasy, or spicy foods if heartburn persists.
vitaminRecommendations: Continue your prenatal vitamin. Iron levels heading into delivery are crucial for managing normal postpartum blood loss and supporting recovery. Continue iron supplementation as prescribed. Omega-3 DHA (200-300 mg) continues to support infant brain development. Choline (450 mg) supports the brain. Calcium (1,200-1,500 mg) and vitamin D (600-2,000 IU) maintain maternal bone health. Magnesium (350-360 mg) may help with sleep and muscle function as labor approaches. Iodine (150-220 mcg) for upcoming breastfeeding. Confirm with your provider whether to continue or stop any pregnancy-specific medications (low-dose aspirin, progesterone) вЂ” typically these are stopped by 36-37 weeks. GBS-positive mothers should go to the hospital in adequate time for IV antibiotics (typically at least 4 hours before delivery). Discuss postpartum supplementation with your provider вЂ” many recommend continuing prenatal vitamins for 6 months postpartum, especially if breastfeeding.
symptomsCommon: Full term: labor could start at any moment. Key labor signs include: regular, progressively intensifying contractions; water breaking (rupture of membranes вЂ” may be a gush or steady trickle); bloody show (blood-tinged mucus discharge); lower back pain that comes in waves and doesn't relent with position changes. The 5-1-1 rule: contractions 5 minutes apart, lasting 1 minute each, for at least 1 hour вЂ” time to go to the hospital or birthing center. Contact your provider immediately if: water breaks (note time and fluid color вЂ” it should be clear; green or brown fluid can indicate meconium and needs urgent evaluation), bleeding (more than light spotting), severe abdominal pain, or significantly decreased fetal movement. Prodromal labor can be confusing and exhausting вЂ” rest whenever possible. The baby should continue moving regularly. Emotional readiness fluctuates. Trust your body and your preparation. You are about to meet your baby.

babySize: the size of a large pumpkin
babyDevelopment: The fetus measures 500 to 520 millimeters head to heel and weighs about 3,500 grams (7 pounds 11 ounces), roughly the size of a large pumpkin. You have reached the estimated due date. About 5 percent of babies are born exactly on their due date; approximately 50 percent are born within a week of it. The fetus is fully mature and ready for life outside the womb. All organ systems function optimally, and the fetus has sufficient fat stores вЂ” about 15 to 16 percent of body weight вЂ” to maintain temperature and provide energy through the initial days when feeding is being established. The brain has developed approximately 100 billion neurons and over 100 trillion synaptic connections, forming the neural substrate for all future learning, memory, emotion, and behavior. Myelination, the insulation of nerve fibers, will continue actively for the first two years and more slowly into early adulthood. The skull bones are designed to slide over each other during delivery вЂ” molding вЂ” allowing the head to pass through the birth canal. The fontanelles (soft spots) close gradually: the posterior by 2 to 3 months, the anterior by 18 months. The eyes can now focus at 8 to 12 inches вЂ” precisely the nursing distance. The first breath triggers a cascade of circulatory changes establishing independent oxygenation. The umbilical cord will be clamped and cut after birth, and the placental site will heal over following weeks. Your baby is ready to meet you.
motherChanges: You have reached your due date. About 50 percent of women deliver by 40 weeks and 5 days. Most providers do not allow pregnancy to continue beyond 41 to 42 weeks due to increased risks associated with post-term pregnancy, including placental aging and decreased amniotic fluid. If labor has not begun, your provider will discuss options: expectant management (waiting for spontaneous labor with increased monitoring), membrane sweeping (a procedure to stimulate labor), or induction of labor. The physical symptoms of late pregnancy are at their maximum: difficulty walking, sleeping, and finding any comfortable position. The baby is deeply engaged for most women, causing intense pelvic pressure, frequent urination, and difficult mobility. Emotionally, the wait can feel endless. Many women feel frustrated, impatient, and physically exhausted. It's normal to be emotional and anxious about when labor will begin. Remember that the due date is an estimate вЂ” only 5 percent of babies arrive exactly on time. Your body and baby know what to do. Distract yourself with gentle activities: movies, reading, light walks, time with loved ones. Rest whenever possible вЂ” labor requires significant physical energy, and starting well-rested is ideal. You will meet your baby very soon.
nutritionTips: Maintain light, energy-sustaining nutrition. Labor is physically demanding, and adequate fuel throughout early labor improves stamina and outcomes. Easily digestible foods: oatmeal, toast with nut butter, yogurt, smoothies, soups, crackers, fruit, eggs. Complex carbohydrates provide sustained energy. Protein supports endurance. Small amounts frequently tolerate better than large meals. Water at 10 to 12 glasses daily вЂ” dehydration can cause false contractions. Electrolyte drinks, coconut water, or diluted juice support hydration. Early labor at home: eat light, easily digested foods to maintain energy. Pack hospital snacks: honey sticks, granola bars, crackers, dried fruit, applesauce pouches. Discuss your provider's policy on eating during labor вЂ” many allow clear liquids and light snacks in early labor. If induction is scheduled, eat a light meal beforehand unless instructed otherwise. Focus on nutrient-dense, easily digested options. Continue to stay well-hydrated.
vitaminRecommendations: Continue your prenatal vitamin. Iron stores heading into delivery are crucial for managing normal postpartum blood loss (500 ml for vaginal delivery, 1,000 ml for cesarean) and supporting recovery. Omega-3 DHA (200-300 mg) supports infant brain development вЂ” consider continuing through breastfeeding. Choline (450 mg) supports the baby's brain. Continue calcium (1,200-1,500 mg) and vitamin D (600-2,000 IU) for maternal bone health, especially important during breastfeeding when calcium demands are high. Magnesium (350-360 mg) may support muscle function during labor. Iodine (150-220 mcg) for breastfeeding. Discuss postpartum supplementation with your provider вЂ” continuing prenatal vitamins for 6 months postpartum is a common recommendation. If you had gestational diabetes, discuss postpartum glucose monitoring. GBS-positive mothers should receive IV antibiotics for at least 4 hours before delivery when possible.
symptomsCommon: Your due date has arrived. Labor signs to watch for: regular, progressively intensifying contractions (the 5-1-1 rule); water breaking (rupture of membranes вЂ” clear fluid, note time and color); bloody show; lower back pain that comes in waves. Contact your provider for: water breaking, decreased fetal movement, heavy bleeding, severe pain, or contractions meeting the 5-1-1 rule. If labor has not started, your provider may discuss: membrane sweeping (separating the amniotic sac from the cervix to stimulate prostaglandin release), expectant management with increased fetal monitoring (non-stress tests, biophysical profiles), or scheduling an induction. The baby should continue moving regularly вЂ” any significant decrease requires immediate evaluation. Prodromal labor and false labor can continue вЂ” rest when possible, stay hydrated, and try to conserve energy. The physical discomforts are at their maximum, and emotional impatience is completely normal. Distract yourself, rest, and trust the process. Your baby will arrive very soon.
